quiz.title: Unit 8 Quiz 3: Monetary Policy Transmission and Crisis
quiz.subtitle: Overnight Rate Effects, Recession, Inflation, and Financial Crises
quiz.subject: Economics
quiz.topic: Monetary Policy Transmission, Recession Fighting, Inflation Fighting, Financial Crises

questions.question: When the overnight loans rate ______ relative to the long-term bond rate, the real GDP growth rate usually ______.
options: rises; slows within one month
rises; slows about one year later
falls; slows about one year later
falls; rises within one month
questions.explanation: When the overnight rate rises (tight monetary policy), GDP growth typically slows with long and variable lags — monetary policy affects the economy about one year later.

questions.question: Suppose the Bank of Canada raises the overnight loans rate. Other short-term interest rates and the exchange rate ______. The quantity of money and supply of loanable funds ______.
options: rise; decrease
fall; decrease
rise; increase
fall; increase
questions.explanation: Raising the overnight rate pulls up other short-term rates and appreciates the currency. The money supply and loanable funds decrease as reserves are drained.

questions.question: Suppose the Bank of Canada raises the overnight loans rate. The long-term real interest rate ____________
options: falls
rises
remains the same
any of the three is possible
questions.explanation: Raising the overnight rate typically increases long-term real interest rates as well, though the effect may be smaller than on short-term rates.

questions.question: A rise in the overnight loan rate ______ reserves and ______ the quantity of deposits and bank loans created.
options: increases; decreases
increases; increases
decreases; increases
decreases; decreases
questions.explanation: A rise in the overnight loan rate typically results from a drain in reserves. Higher rates reduce bank lending and deposit creation, decreasing the quantity of loans.

questions.question: The quantity of money demanded ______. Consumers and firms spend ______.
options: decreases; more
decreases; less
increases; more
increases; less
questions.explanation: When interest rates rise, the quantity of money demanded decreases (opportunity cost of holding money increases). Higher rates also reduce borrowing and spending.

questions.question: When the Bank of Canada fights recession, _______.
options: an open market sale increases the overnight loans rate
an increase in monetary base increases the demand for money
an increase in the supply of loanable funds lowers the long-term interest rate and increases investment
an increase in the overnight loans rate increases aggregate demand
questions.explanation: To fight recession, the Bank expands the monetary base, increasing loanable funds supply. This lowers long-term interest rates, stimulating investment and aggregate demand.

questions.question: When the Bank of Canada fights inflation, _______.
options: a decrease in bank reserves decreases the quantity of money demanded
an open market purchase increases the overnight loans rate
a decrease in the federal funds rate decreases aggregate demand
a decrease in the supply of loanable funds raises the long-term interest rate and increases investment
questions.explanation: To fight inflation, the Bank reduces bank reserves, raising interest rates. Higher rates decrease the quantity of money demanded and reduce aggregate demand.

questions.question: When the overnight loans rate falls relative to the long-term bond rate, _______.
options: real GDP growth slows one year later
the Bank of Canada is trying to restrain inflation and slow real GDP growth
real GDP growth speeds up in less than one month
the Bank of Canada is trying to stimulate real GDP growth
questions.explanation: A falling overnight rate (relative to bond rates) indicates expansionary monetary policy, which aims to stimulate real GDP growth by lowering borrowing costs.

questions.question: The three ingredients of a financial and banking crisis are _______.
options: a widespread fall in asset prices, a large currency drain, and a run on a bank
a widespread fall in asset prices, a fall in the foreign exchange rate, and a significant fall in interest rates
a fall in the foreign exchange rate, a significant fall in interest rates, and over-zealous bank regulations
over-zealous bank regulations, a fall in the foreign exchange rate, and a large currency drain
questions.explanation: The classic ingredients of a financial crisis are: (1) widespread fall in asset prices, (2) large currency drain (capital flight), and (3) a run on banks (depositor panic).

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! Strong grasp of monetary policy transmission and crisis dynamics.
scoreMessages.70: Good work. Review how interest rates affect money demand and spending.
scoreMessages.50: Solid effort. Focus on expansionary vs contractionary policy effects.
scoreMessages.0: Keep studying. Lower rates stimulate growth; higher rates fight inflation.
